pillar.metaDescription: Compare Medicare Advantage (Part C) plans, costs, and coverage for 2026. Talk to a licensed advisor free at 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) today.
pillar.h1: Medicare Advantage Plans: The Complete 2026 Guide
pillar.quickAnswer: Medicare Advantage (Part C) is private, Medicare-approved insurance that bundles Part A, Part B, and usually Part D drug coverage into one plan, often with $0 premiums and dental, vision, and hearing benefits. In 2026, CMS reports roughly 34 million enrollees, over half of all Medicare beneficiaries.
pillar.intro: Medicare Advantage replaces Original Medicare with an all-in-one private plan. Here is how Part C works, what it covers, and how to choose the right plan for 2026.
pillar.heroSubhead: One number for every senior need. Compare Medicare Advantage plans with a licensed advisor at 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: What is Medicare Advantage (Part C)?
sections.body: Medicare Advantage, also called Part C, is an alternative to Original Medicare offered by private insurers approved by the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS). When you enroll, the plan provides all your Part A (hospital) and Part B (medical) benefits, and most plans add Part D prescription drug coverage plus extras like dental, vision, hearing, and fitness memberships. You still pay your Part B premium, but the plan handles your claims instead of the federal government. Plans use provider networks (HMO or PPO) and set their own copays, coinsurance, and annual out-of-pocket maximums. To compare your local options, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

sections.h2: How much does Medicare Advantage cost in 2026?
sections.body: Many Medicare Advantage plans advertise a $0 monthly premium, but $0 premium never means $0 cost. You still pay your Part B premium ($185 per month in 2026 for most people), plus copays, coinsurance, and deductibles when you use care. The trade-off for low premiums is an annual out-of-pocket maximum; in 2026 the federal cap for in-network services is $9,250, though many plans set lower limits. Drug costs follow the plan's Part D formulary. Because pricing and networks vary widely by ZIP code, a licensed advisor at 1-800-MEDIGAP can model your real annual cost based on your doctors and medications.

sections.h2: Medicare Advantage vs. Original Medicare with Medigap
sections.body: The core choice for new beneficiaries is Medicare Advantage versus Original Medicare paired with a Medigap (Medicare Supplement) policy. Medicare Advantage typically costs less upfront and bundles extras, but limits you to a network and requires prior authorization for many services. Original Medicare plus Medigap costs more in monthly premiums but lets you see any U.S. doctor who accepts Medicare with little to no claim paperwork and predictable bills. Roughly half of beneficiaries choose each path. The right answer depends on your health, budget, travel, and whether you want freedom of provider choice. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for an unbiased side-by-side comparison.

sections.h2: Pros and cons of Medicare Advantage
sections.body: Pros: lower or $0 premiums, an all-in-one plan, a built-in out-of-pocket maximum that Original Medicare lacks, and extra benefits like dental, vision, hearing, and over-the-counter allowances. Cons: provider networks restrict which doctors and hospitals you can use, referrals and prior authorization can delay care, coverage is mostly local so travel may not be covered, and plans change benefits and networks every year. According to KFF, prior authorization requirements are the most common complaint. Weighing these trade-offs against your own health needs is exactly what the licensed advisors at 1-800-MEDIGAP do at no cost to you.

sections.h2: When can you enroll in or change a Medicare Advantage plan?
sections.body: You can first join Medicare Advantage during your seven-month Initial Enrollment Period around your 65th birthday. After that, the Annual Election Period (October 15 to December 7) lets anyone join, switch, or drop a plan for the following year. The Medicare Advantage Open Enrollment Period (January 1 to March 31) lets current Advantage members switch plans once or return to Original Medicare. Special Enrollment Periods apply after moving, losing other coverage, or other qualifying events. Missing these windows can mean waiting a full year, so call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to confirm your eligibility dates.

faqs.q: Is Medicare Advantage the same as Medicare Part C?
faqs.a: Yes. Medicare Advantage and Medicare Part C are two names for the same program. Part C is the legal name in Medicare law; Medicare Advantage is the marketing name CMS and insurers use. Both refer to private, Medicare-approved plans that bundle Part A and Part B, usually with Part D drug coverage included.

faqs.q: Does Medicare Advantage replace Original Medicare?
faqs.a: Functionally, yes. While you stay enrolled in Medicare, a Medicare Advantage plan provides your Part A and Part B benefits through a private insurer instead of the federal government. You use the plan's network and card for care. You can return to Original Medicare during specific enrollment periods if the plan no longer fits your needs.

faqs.q: Do I still pay the Part B premium with Medicare Advantage?
faqs.a: Yes. You must keep paying your monthly Part B premium ($185 in 2026 for most people) even on a $0 premium Medicare Advantage plan. The plan premium, if any, is separate. Some plans offer a giveback benefit that reduces your Part B premium, which a 1-800-MEDIGAP advisor can help you find.

faqs.q: Does Medicare Advantage cover prescription drugs?
faqs.a: Most Medicare Advantage plans (called MA-PD plans) include Part D prescription drug coverage built in, so you do not need a separate drug plan. A small number of plans, often those paired with separate drug coverage, do not. Always check the plan's drug formulary to confirm your medications are covered before enrolling.

faqs.q: Can I have both Medicare Advantage and a Medigap plan?
faqs.a: No. You cannot use a Medigap (Medicare Supplement) policy with a Medicare Advantage plan, and it is illegal for anyone to sell you one. Medigap only works with Original Medicare. If you want supplemental coverage, you must choose one path or the other. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to decide which fits you best.

faqs.q: How many Medicare Advantage plans can I choose from?
faqs.a: It varies by location. CMS reports the average beneficiary can choose from dozens of Medicare Advantage plans in 2026, with availability and benefits differing by county and ZIP code. Because the menu is so large and changes yearly, a licensed advisor at 1-800-MEDIGAP can narrow it to plans that cover your doctors and drugs.

faqs.q: What is a Medicare Advantage star rating?
faqs.a: CMS rates every Medicare Advantage plan from 1 to 5 stars each year based on quality, member satisfaction, and customer service. Five stars is excellent; anything below 3 is below average. Higher-rated plans tend to deliver better care coordination and service. Star ratings are a free, objective tool you can use when comparing plans.

faqs.q: How do I find the best Medicare Advantage plan for me?
faqs.a: The best plan is the one that covers your specific doctors, hospitals, and medications at the lowest total annual cost, not just the lowest premium. Compare networks, drug formularies, out-of-pocket maximums, extra benefits, and star ratings. A free call to 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) connects you with a licensed advisor who compares plans side by side.

page.intro: Medicare Advantage is the private alternative to Original Medicare. Here is what it covers, how it works, and how to decide if it fits you.
page.heroSubhead: Compare Medicare Advantage plans with a licensed advisor at 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: How does Medicare Advantage work?
sections.body: When you join a Medicare Advantage plan, a private insurer approved by CMS takes over your Medicare benefits. You keep paying your Part B premium, and the plan handles your Part A and Part B care, usually adding Part D drug coverage and extras. You use the plan's network of doctors and hospitals, pay copays at the point of care, and are protected by an annual out-of-pocket maximum. Plans renew yearly, and benefits can change, so it pays to review your coverage each fall. Questions? Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

sections.h2: What does Medicare Advantage cover?
sections.body: Every Medicare Advantage plan must cover everything Original Medicare covers, including hospital stays, doctor visits, lab work, and preventive care. Beyond that, most plans add prescription drugs, routine dental, vision, hearing aids, fitness memberships, and over-the-counter allowances that Original Medicare does not include. Some plans offer transportation, meal delivery after a hospital stay, or a Part B premium giveback. Benefits vary by plan and location, so confirm the specifics before enrolling. A licensed advisor at 1-800-MEDIGAP can match the extras you want to plans available in your ZIP code.

sections.h2: Is Medicare Advantage right for you?
sections.body: Medicare Advantage works well if you want low premiums, bundled benefits, and an out-of-pocket cap, and you are comfortable using a network. It may be a weaker fit if you travel often, want any-doctor freedom, or take specialty drugs that need prior authorization. The decision hinges on your health, budget, and preferences, not on advertising. Because the trade-offs are personal, the free advisors at 1-800-MEDIGAP review your doctors, medications, and priorities before recommending a plan, with no pressure and no cost to you.

faqs.q: What is the main downside of Medicare Advantage?
faqs.a: The biggest downside is restricted access. Most plans use networks, so seeing out-of-network doctors costs more or is not covered, and many services require prior authorization that can delay care. Coverage is also mostly local, which can complicate travel. Weigh these against the lower premiums and extra benefits.

faqs.q: Can I keep my own doctor with Medicare Advantage?
faqs.a: Only if your doctor is in the plan's network. HMO plans generally require in-network providers; PPO plans allow out-of-network care at higher cost. Always verify your doctors and hospitals are in-network before enrolling. A 1-800-MEDIGAP advisor can check this for you for free.

faqs.q: How much does Medicare Advantage cost?
faqs.a: Many plans have a $0 monthly premium, but you still pay your Part B premium ($185 in 2026 for most), plus copays and coinsurance when you use care. Your total cost depends on how often you need services and your plan's out-of-pocket maximum, which is capped at $9,250 in-network in 2026.

faqs.q: Is Medicare Advantage good or bad?
faqs.a: Neither universally. Medicare Advantage is a good fit for people who value low premiums and bundled benefits and don't mind a network, and a poor fit for those who want any-doctor freedom or travel often. The right answer depends on your situation. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for an honest assessment.

faqs.q: How do I switch or enroll in Medicare Advantage?
faqs.a: You can join during your Initial Enrollment Period at 65, the Annual Election Period (October 15 to December 7), or the Medicare Advantage Open Enrollment Period (January 1 to March 31). Special Enrollment Periods cover qualifying life events. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to confirm your dates and compare plans.

page.metaDescription: What is Medicare Part C? Learn how Part C / Medicare Advantage works, costs, and coverage. Free help from 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.quickAnswer: Medicare Part C, better known as Medicare Advantage, is the part of Medicare law that lets private insurers offer bundled, Medicare-approved plans. Part C combines Part A and Part B, usually adds Part D drug coverage, and is regulated by CMS, which reports about 34 million enrollees in 2026.
page.intro: Medicare Part C is the official name for Medicare Advantage. Here is what Part C means and how it fits into the Medicare alphabet.
page.heroSubhead: Understand Part C with a free call to 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: What is Medicare Part C?
sections.body: Medicare Part C is the section of federal law that authorizes private companies to offer Medicare Advantage plans. The two terms are interchangeable: Part C is the legal label and Medicare Advantage is the brand name you see in ads. A Part C plan must provide all the benefits of Part A (hospital) and Part B (medical), and most also include Part D (prescription drugs) along with extras like dental and vision. Instead of the government paying your claims, a CMS-approved insurer manages your care through a network. To see Part C plans in your area, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

sections.h2: How Part C fits with Parts A, B, and D
sections.body: Medicare has four parts. Part A covers hospital care and Part B covers doctor visits; together they form Original Medicare. Part D covers prescription drugs. Part C, or Medicare Advantage, is the private bundle that rolls A, B, and usually D into a single plan. You cannot have Part C without first being enrolled in Parts A and B, and you keep paying your Part B premium. Think of Part C as a different way to receive your Medicare benefits rather than an add-on. A 1-800-MEDIGAP advisor can map out how the parts work for your situation.

faqs.q: Is Medicare Part C the same as Medicare Advantage?
faqs.a: Yes. Medicare Part C and Medicare Advantage are identical. Part C is the name written into Medicare law, while Medicare Advantage is the consumer-facing name CMS and insurers use in marketing. Both describe private, Medicare-approved plans that bundle your Part A, Part B, and usually Part D coverage.

faqs.q: Do I need Part A and Part B to get Part C?
faqs.a: Yes. You must be enrolled in both Medicare Part A and Part B before you can join a Part C (Medicare Advantage) plan, and you continue paying your Part B premium. The Part C plan then provides your Part A and Part B benefits through a private insurer instead of the government.

faqs.q: Does Medicare Part C include drug coverage?
faqs.a: Most Part C plans include Part D prescription drug coverage built in, so you don't need a separate drug plan. These are called MA-PD plans. A few Part C plans exclude drug coverage. Always confirm the plan covers your specific medications before you enroll.

faqs.q: How much does Medicare Part C cost?
faqs.a: Part C plan premiums range from $0 to over $100 monthly, but you always keep paying your Part B premium ($185 in 2026 for most people). You also pay copays and coinsurance when you use care, capped by an annual out-of-pocket maximum. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for a personalized cost estimate.

faqs.q: Why is it called Part C?
faqs.a: It's called Part C because Medicare added it as the third major component after Part A (hospital) and Part B (medical) in the alphabet of benefits. Congress created Part C in 1997 to let private insurers offer bundled Medicare plans, later branded Medicare Advantage.

CMS reports about 34 million people, over half of Medicare beneficiaries, are enrolled in 2026.

sections.h2: Medicare Advantage in plain English
sections.body: Medicare Advantage is a way to get your Medicare benefits through a private insurance company instead of directly from the federal government. The insurer, approved and paid by CMS, gives you everything Original Medicare covers and usually adds prescription drugs, dental, vision, and hearing benefits in a single plan with one card. You keep paying your Part B premium, use the plan's network of doctors, and pay copays as you go. In exchange for using a network, you often get lower premiums and an annual cap on what you spend. To see what's available where you live, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

sections.h2: How is it different from Original Medicare?
sections.body: With Original Medicare, the government pays your claims and you can see any provider nationwide who accepts Medicare, but there is no cap on out-of-pocket spending and no drug or dental coverage built in. With Medicare Advantage, a private plan manages your care through a network, adds extra benefits, and includes an out-of-pocket maximum, but you trade some freedom of choice. Neither is automatically better; the right choice depends on your health, budget, and how you use care. A free, unbiased call to 1-800-MEDIGAP helps you weigh both paths clearly.

faqs.q: What is Medicare Advantage in simple terms?
faqs.a: Medicare Advantage is private health insurance that gives you your Medicare benefits in one bundled plan, usually including drugs, dental, and vision. A company approved by Medicare manages your care through a network of doctors, often for a low or $0 premium, with an annual cap on your costs.

faqs.q: Is Medicare Advantage real Medicare?
faqs.a: Yes. Medicare Advantage is an official part of the Medicare program, authorized by federal law as Part C. You remain enrolled in Medicare, but a private insurer approved by CMS delivers your benefits instead of the government. It must cover everything Original Medicare covers.

faqs.q: What does Medicare Advantage cost?
faqs.a: Many Medicare Advantage plans have $0 premiums, but you still pay your Part B premium ($185 in 2026 for most people) plus copays when you use care. Your annual spending is capped at $9,250 in-network in 2026. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for a personalized estimate.

faqs.q: Who should consider Medicare Advantage?
faqs.a: Medicare Advantage suits people who want low premiums, bundled benefits like dental and drugs, and an out-of-pocket cap, and who don't mind using a provider network. It's less ideal for frequent travelers or those who want any-doctor freedom. A 1-800-MEDIGAP advisor can tell you if it fits.

faqs.q: Does Medicare Advantage cost more than Original Medicare?
faqs.a: Upfront, Medicare Advantage usually costs less because premiums are often $0. However, copays and coinsurance can add up if you need a lot of care. Original Medicare with Medigap costs more monthly but offers more predictable bills. The total cost depends on your health needs.

page.metaDescription: How to find the best Medicare Advantage plans for 2026 based on your doctors, drugs, and budget. Compare free at 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.quickAnswer: The best Medicare Advantage plan for 2026 is the one that covers your specific doctors, hospitals, and medications at the lowest total annual cost, not just the lowest premium. Key factors are network, drug formulary, out-of-pocket maximum, extra benefits, and the CMS 1-to-5 star rating.
page.intro: There is no single best Medicare Advantage plan for everyone. Here is how to find the best 2026 plan for your needs.
page.heroSubhead: Compare the top-rated 2026 plans for your ZIP code at 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: What makes a Medicare Advantage plan the best?
sections.body: The best plan is personal. A plan that's perfect for your neighbor could be wrong for you because it doesn't include your cardiologist or cover your insulin. To judge any 2026 plan, check five things: is your doctor in-network, are your drugs on the formulary at a good tier, how low is the out-of-pocket maximum, what extra benefits matter to you (dental, vision, giveback), and what is the plan's CMS star rating. A plan scoring well on all five for your situation is your best plan. The free advisors at 1-800-MEDIGAP run this comparison for you at no cost.

sections.h2: How to compare 2026 plans the smart way
sections.body: Start with your providers and prescriptions, not the advertising. List your doctors, hospitals, and exact medications, then filter plans that cover all of them. Next, compare total estimated annual cost (premiums plus expected copays), not just the monthly premium, since a $0 premium plan can cost more if it has high copays. Finally, favor plans with 4 or 5 CMS stars for better service and care coordination. This is detailed work, and it changes every year, which is why a licensed 1-800-MEDIGAP advisor does the legwork and presents your best options side by side.

faqs.q: Which Medicare Advantage plan is the best for 2026?
faqs.a: There is no single best plan. The best 2026 Medicare Advantage plan is the one that covers your doctors and medications at the lowest total annual cost, with strong CMS star ratings. Because plans and networks vary by ZIP code, call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare the top plans where you live.

faqs.q: What are the highest-rated Medicare Advantage plans?
faqs.a: The highest-rated plans earn 5 stars from CMS for quality, member satisfaction, and service. Star ratings are published each fall and vary by insurer and region. A 5-star plan also allows special enrollment year-round. A 1-800-MEDIGAP advisor can identify the top-rated plans in your area.

faqs.q: Should I pick the plan with the lowest premium?
faqs.a: Not necessarily. A $0 or low-premium plan can still cost more overall if it has high copays, a limited network, or doesn't cover your drugs well. Compare total estimated annual cost, not just the premium. The lowest-premium plan is rarely the cheapest once you factor in care.

faqs.q: How often do Medicare Advantage plans change?
faqs.a: Plans update their premiums, networks, drug formularies, and benefits every calendar year. A plan that was best in 2025 may not be best in 2026. That's why CMS gives you the Annual Election Period (October 15 to December 7) to review and switch. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP each fall to recheck.

faqs.q: Is it free to compare Medicare Advantage plans?
faqs.a: Yes. Comparing plans through licensed advisors at 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) is completely free, with no obligation. Advisors are paid by the insurers, so you pay nothing for the help, and your plan premium is the same whether you enroll with an advisor or on your own.

page.metaDescription: Medicare Advantage vs Original Medicare: compare cost, coverage, networks, and freedom. Get a free side-by-side from 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.quickAnswer: Medicare Advantage bundles your benefits through a private plan with low premiums, a network, and extras, plus an out-of-pocket cap. Original Medicare lets you see any U.S. provider who accepts Medicare with no network, but has no spending cap and no built-in drugs or dental. Roughly half of beneficiaries choose each.
page.intro: Choosing between Medicare Advantage and Original Medicare? Here is a clear, side-by-side comparison of the two paths.
page.heroSubhead: Get a free side-by-side comparison from 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: The core differences at a glance
sections.body: Original Medicare is the government-run program: Parts A and B let you use any doctor or hospital nationwide that accepts Medicare, with no networks and no referrals, but no out-of-pocket cap and no built-in drug, dental, or vision coverage. Medicare Advantage is a private bundle that adds those extras and an annual spending cap and usually charges a low or $0 premium, but restricts you to a network and often requires prior authorization. Most people add a Medigap policy to Original Medicare to fill its gaps. Which path wins depends entirely on you. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for a personalized comparison.

sections.h2: Which one should you choose?
sections.body: Choose Medicare Advantage if you want the lowest premiums, all-in-one convenience, extra benefits, and you're comfortable with a local network. Choose Original Medicare (usually with Medigap and a Part D plan) if you want to see any doctor in the country, travel freely, avoid prior-authorization hassles, and prefer predictable bills, accepting higher monthly premiums in return. Your health status, budget, travel habits, and provider loyalty all matter. Because this is one of the most important decisions in retirement, the licensed advisors at 1-800-MEDIGAP walk you through both options with no sales pressure.

faqs.q: Is Medicare Advantage better than Original Medicare?
faqs.a: Neither is universally better. Medicare Advantage offers lower premiums, bundled extras, and a spending cap but limits you to a network. Original Medicare offers nationwide provider freedom and predictable bills (with Medigap) but costs more monthly. The better choice depends on your health, budget, and travel needs.

faqs.q: Can I switch from Original Medicare to Medicare Advantage?
faqs.a: Yes. You can switch from Original Medicare to a Medicare Advantage plan during the Annual Election Period (October 15 to December 7) or your Initial Enrollment Period at 65. The change takes effect January 1. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to confirm your eligibility and compare plans.

faqs.q: Does Original Medicare have an out-of-pocket maximum?
faqs.a: No. Original Medicare has no annual out-of-pocket maximum, so your costs are theoretically unlimited without supplemental coverage. That's why most Original Medicare beneficiaries add a Medigap policy. Medicare Advantage, by contrast, includes a federal out-of-pocket cap of $9,250 in-network in 2026.

faqs.q: Do I need a referral with Original Medicare?
faqs.a: No. Original Medicare does not require referrals to see specialists, and there are no networks, so you can visit any provider nationwide who accepts Medicare. Many Medicare Advantage HMO plans, by contrast, require referrals and in-network providers for coverage.

faqs.q: Can I have drug coverage with Original Medicare?
faqs.a: Yes, but not automatically. Original Medicare does not include drug coverage, so you add a standalone Part D plan. Medicare Advantage plans usually build drug coverage in. If you choose Original Medicare, a 1-800-MEDIGAP advisor can help you pick a Part D plan that covers your medications.

page.metaDescription: The real disadvantages of Medicare Advantage: networks, prior authorization, and more. Get honest guidance from 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.quickAnswer: The main disadvantages of Medicare Advantage are restricted provider networks, frequent prior-authorization requirements, mostly local coverage that limits travel, and yearly changes to benefits and networks. KFF research identifies prior authorization as the top member complaint, and you cannot pair an Advantage plan with Medigap.
page.intro: Medicare Advantage has real trade-offs. Here are the honest disadvantages to weigh before you enroll.

sections.h2: The biggest disadvantages of Medicare Advantage
sections.body: Networks are the first drawback: most plans only fully cover in-network doctors and hospitals, so a favorite specialist may be off-limits or cost more. Prior authorization is the second: plans often require approval before covering services, which KFF reports is the leading source of member frustration and can delay care. Third, coverage is largely local, so extended travel or a move can leave you exposed. Fourth, plans change premiums, networks, and drug formularies every year, forcing an annual review. Finally, switching back to Medigap later can require medical underwriting. A 1-800-MEDIGAP advisor explains how each applies to you.

sections.h2: Who should think twice about Medicare Advantage?
sections.body: Medicare Advantage may not suit you if you travel frequently or split time between states, since networks are local. It's also worth caution if you have a serious or chronic condition that needs specialists or expensive treatments, because prior authorization and networks can complicate access. If you value the freedom to see any Medicare doctor nationwide and want predictable bills, Original Medicare with a Medigap plan may serve you better despite higher premiums. The right answer is personal, and the free advisors at 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) help you weigh it without sales pressure.

faqs.q: What is the biggest disadvantage of Medicare Advantage?
faqs.a: The biggest disadvantage is restricted access to care through provider networks and prior-authorization rules. Most plans only fully cover in-network doctors, and many services require plan approval first, which KFF identifies as the top member complaint and can delay needed treatment.

faqs.q: Why do doctors not like Medicare Advantage?
faqs.a: Some doctors dislike Medicare Advantage because of administrative burdens like prior-authorization requirements, claim denials, and slower or lower reimbursement compared with Original Medicare. These hassles can lead some providers to limit or stop accepting certain Advantage plans, which is why verifying your doctor's participation matters.

faqs.q: Can I leave Medicare Advantage if I don't like it?
faqs.a: Yes. You can switch plans or return to Original Medicare during the Medicare Advantage Open Enrollment Period (January 1 to March 31) or the Annual Election Period (October 15 to December 7). Note that adding Medigap later may require medical underwriting. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for guidance.

faqs.q: Does Medicare Advantage cover you when traveling?
faqs.a: Usually only for emergencies. Most Medicare Advantage plans cover routine care only within their local network, so non-emergency care while traveling out of the area may not be covered. If you travel often, Original Medicare with Medigap offers broader nationwide access. A 1-800-MEDIGAP advisor can compare.

faqs.q: Is it hard to switch from Medicare Advantage back to Medigap?
faqs.a: It can be. Outside of guaranteed-issue situations, switching from Medicare Advantage to Original Medicare with a Medigap policy may require medical underwriting, meaning insurers can charge more or deny coverage based on health. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn whether you qualify for guaranteed-issue rights.

page.quickAnswer: Medicare Advantage availability, networks, and benefits are set by county and ZIP code, so the plans near you differ from those a few miles away. To find local plans, compare each insurer's network and drug formulary against your own doctors and medications, or call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare instantly.
page.intro: Medicare Advantage plans are local. Here is how to find and compare the plans available in your area.
page.heroSubhead: Find plans in your ZIP code free at 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: Why Medicare Advantage plans are local
sections.body: Unlike Original Medicare, which works the same nationwide, Medicare Advantage plans are built around regional provider networks and are offered county by county. CMS approves which plans an insurer can sell in each area, so your ZIP code determines your menu of choices, the doctors in-network, and even the extra benefits offered. A plan available in one town may not exist in the next county. This is why searching 'plans near me' matters and why the comparison must be done at the local level. The fastest way is a free call to 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) with your ZIP code ready.

sections.h2: How to compare local plans the right way
sections.body: Have three things ready: your ZIP code, a list of your doctors and hospitals, and your exact medications. Then check each local plan to confirm your providers are in-network and your drugs are on the formulary, compare the out-of-pocket maximums and extra benefits, and look at CMS star ratings for service quality. Doing this across every plan in your area by hand is time-consuming, and the plans change yearly. A licensed advisor at 1-800-MEDIGAP runs the local comparison for you in minutes, at no cost, and presents your best matches side by side.

faqs.q: How do I find Medicare Advantage plans in my area?
faqs.a: Medicare Advantage plans are offered by ZIP code and county, so use your local ZIP to filter available plans. Check each plan's network and drug formulary against your doctors and medications. The quickest way is to call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) and compare every local plan with a licensed advisor.

faqs.q: Why are different plans available in different ZIP codes?
faqs.a: CMS approves Medicare Advantage plans on a county-by-county basis, and insurers build local provider networks. As a result, the plans, networks, and extra benefits available depend on exactly where you live. A plan offered in one ZIP code may be unavailable in a neighboring one.

faqs.q: Are Medicare Advantage networks local?
faqs.a: Yes. Most Medicare Advantage plans use local or regional networks of doctors and hospitals. Routine care outside the network often isn't covered, except emergencies. If you move or travel often, confirm coverage carefully or consider Original Medicare with Medigap for nationwide access.

faqs.q: What information do I need to compare local plans?
faqs.a: Have your ZIP code, a list of your doctors and hospitals, and your exact medications ready. With those, an advisor can confirm which local plans cover your providers and drugs and compare costs, benefits, and star ratings. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP and have these details handy.

faqs.q: Does it cost anything to compare plans near me?
faqs.a: No. Comparing Medicare Advantage plans through 1-800-MEDIGAP is completely free with no obligation. Licensed advisors are compensated by insurers, so your plan premium is identical whether you enroll with help or on your own. You simply get expert guidance at no cost.

page.metaDescription: When can you enroll in Medicare Advantage? Key 2026 enrollment periods and deadlines explained. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to enroll free.
page.quickAnswer: You can enroll in Medicare Advantage during your 7-month Initial Enrollment Period at 65, the Annual Election Period (October 15 to December 7), the Medicare Advantage Open Enrollment Period (January 1 to March 31), or a Special Enrollment Period after qualifying events like moving or losing coverage.
page.intro: Timing is everything with Medicare Advantage. Here are the enrollment periods and deadlines you need to know.

sections.h2: The key Medicare Advantage enrollment periods
sections.body: Four windows let you join or change a Medicare Advantage plan. Your Initial Enrollment Period spans the seven months around your 65th birthday and is your first chance to enroll. The Annual Election Period, October 15 to December 7, lets anyone join, switch, or drop a plan for the next year. The Medicare Advantage Open Enrollment Period, January 1 to March 31, lets current members switch once or return to Original Medicare. Special Enrollment Periods open after qualifying life events. Missing your window usually means waiting until the next one, so call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to confirm your exact dates.

sections.h2: Special Enrollment Periods explained
sections.body: Beyond the standard windows, certain life events trigger a Special Enrollment Period that lets you enroll or switch outside the normal calendar. Common triggers include moving out of your plan's service area, losing employer or other creditable coverage, qualifying for Medicaid or Extra Help, or your plan leaving Medicare. Five-star plans also allow a special enrollment window once per year. Each event has its own timeframe, often 60 days, so acting quickly matters. If you've had a recent life change, a licensed advisor at 1-800-MEDIGAP can confirm whether you qualify and help you enroll before the window closes.

faqs.q: When can I enroll in Medicare Advantage?
faqs.a: You can enroll during your Initial Enrollment Period at 65, the Annual Election Period (October 15 to December 7), the Medicare Advantage Open Enrollment Period (January 1 to March 31), or a Special Enrollment Period after a qualifying event. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to confirm which applies to you.

faqs.q: What is the deadline to enroll for 2026 coverage?
faqs.a: For coverage starting January 1, 2026, the Annual Election Period runs October 15 to December 7, 2025. Changes made then take effect at the start of the year. If you miss it, you may have to wait for the next window unless you qualify for a Special Enrollment Period.

faqs.q: Can I change my Medicare Advantage plan after enrolling?
faqs.a: Yes. The Medicare Advantage Open Enrollment Period (January 1 to March 31) lets current members switch plans once or return to Original Medicare. You can also change during the fall Annual Election Period. Special Enrollment Periods may apply after qualifying life events. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for help.

faqs.q: What happens if I miss my enrollment window?
faqs.a: If you miss your enrollment window without a qualifying Special Enrollment Period, you generally must wait until the next Annual Election Period (October 15 to December 7) to join or change a plan. Delaying drug coverage may also trigger a late-enrollment penalty. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to check your options.

faqs.q: Do I automatically get enrolled in Medicare Advantage?
faqs.a: No. Medicare Advantage enrollment is never automatic; you must actively choose and join a plan. If you do nothing at 65, you'll have Original Medicare. To add a Medicare Advantage plan, you must enroll during a valid election period. A 1-800-MEDIGAP advisor can walk you through it.

children.keyword: can i switch from medicare advantage to medigap

page.metaDescription: Can you switch from Medicare Advantage to Medigap? Yes, with timing and underwriting rules. Learn how at 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.h1: Can I Switch From Medicare Advantage to Medigap?
page.quickAnswer: Yes, you can switch from Medicare Advantage to Original Medicare with a Medigap policy, but only during specific windows, and outside guaranteed-issue situations insurers can medically underwrite you, meaning they may charge more or deny coverage based on your health. Timing and your state's rules matter greatly.
page.intro: Switching from Medicare Advantage to Medigap is possible but has rules. Here is what you need to know first.
page.heroSubhead: See if you qualify to switch, free at 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: How the switch works
sections.body: To move from Medicare Advantage to Medigap, you first drop your Advantage plan and return to Original Medicare during a valid window, the Medicare Advantage Open Enrollment Period (January 1 to March 31) or the Annual Election Period (October 15 to December 7). Then you apply for a Medigap policy and usually a standalone Part D drug plan. The catch is that buying Medigap after your initial 6-month Medigap Open Enrollment window often requires medical underwriting, so insurers can review your health. Some situations grant guaranteed-issue rights that waive underwriting. A 1-800-MEDIGAP advisor can tell you which applies to you.

sections.h2: When can you switch without underwriting?
sections.body: Certain protections let you buy Medigap without health questions. If you joined Medicare Advantage when you first turned 65 and switch back to Original Medicare within your first 12 months (the trial right), you have a guaranteed-issue right to Medigap. Other guaranteed-issue triggers include your plan leaving Medicare or moving out of its service area. A few states, like New York and Connecticut, allow year-round guaranteed-issue Medigap. Outside these, underwriting applies. Because the rules are nuanced and state-specific, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to find out exactly when and how you can switch.

faqs.q: Can I switch from Medicare Advantage to Medigap?
faqs.a: Yes. You can drop Medicare Advantage, return to Original Medicare during a valid enrollment window, and buy a Medigap policy. However, outside guaranteed-issue situations, Medigap insurers can use medical underwriting and may charge more or deny coverage based on your health. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to check your options.

faqs.q: Will I have to answer health questions to get Medigap?
faqs.a: Usually yes, unless you have a guaranteed-issue right. Outside your initial 6-month Medigap Open Enrollment, the trial right, or other protected situations, insurers can medically underwrite Medigap applications. A 1-800-MEDIGAP advisor can determine whether you qualify for guaranteed-issue coverage without health questions.

faqs.q: What is the Medicare Advantage trial right?
faqs.a: The trial right lets you try Medicare Advantage when you first join at 65, and if you switch back to Original Medicare within 12 months, you get a guaranteed-issue right to buy Medigap without medical underwriting. It's a one-time protection for first-time Advantage enrollees.

faqs.q: When can I make the switch?
faqs.a: You can return to Original Medicare during the Medicare Advantage Open Enrollment Period (January 1 to March 31) or the fall Annual Election Period (October 15 to December 7). You then apply for Medigap, which may involve underwriting depending on timing. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to plan the switch.

faqs.q: Can a Medigap insurer deny me coverage?
faqs.a: Yes, outside guaranteed-issue periods. If you apply for Medigap with medical underwriting, an insurer can charge higher premiums or deny you based on health conditions. During guaranteed-issue windows, they cannot. Understanding your timing is critical, so call 1-800-MEDIGAP before dropping your Advantage plan.

page.metaDescription: Medicare Advantage HMO vs PPO: compare networks, referrals, and out-of-network costs. Pick the right plan with 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.quickAnswer: A Medicare Advantage HMO requires you to use in-network providers and usually a referral to see specialists, with no out-of-network coverage except emergencies. A PPO lets you see out-of-network providers at higher cost and skip referrals, trading higher premiums for more flexibility.
page.intro: HMO or PPO? The plan type shapes your costs and freedom. Here is how Medicare Advantage HMO and PPO plans compare.
page.heroSubhead: Choose the right plan type with 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: HMO plans: lower cost, tighter network
sections.body: A Medicare Advantage HMO (Health Maintenance Organization) keeps costs low by requiring you to use its network of doctors and hospitals. You typically choose a primary care physician who coordinates your care and provides referrals to see specialists. Outside the network, only emergency and urgent care is covered, so non-emergency out-of-network visits come out of your pocket. In exchange for these restrictions, HMO plans often have the lowest premiums and copays. They suit people who don't travel much and are happy with local in-network providers. To see HMO options near you, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

sections.h2: PPO plans: more freedom, higher cost
sections.body: A Medicare Advantage PPO (Preferred Provider Organization) gives you more flexibility. You can see out-of-network providers, though at a higher cost-share, and you generally don't need referrals to visit specialists. This makes PPOs a better fit for people who travel, want broader provider access, or value the freedom to self-refer. The trade-off is usually higher premiums and copays than a comparable HMO. The right choice depends on how much you value flexibility versus saving money. A licensed advisor at 1-800-MEDIGAP can compare HMO and PPO plans in your area side by side so you pick the best structure for your life.

faqs.q: What is the difference between an HMO and PPO Medicare Advantage plan?
faqs.a: An HMO requires in-network providers and usually referrals, covering out-of-network care only in emergencies, with lower premiums. A PPO lets you see out-of-network providers at higher cost and skip referrals, with more flexibility but typically higher premiums. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare both.

faqs.q: Do I need a referral with a Medicare Advantage HMO?
faqs.a: Usually yes. Most Medicare Advantage HMO plans require you to choose a primary care physician and get a referral before seeing a specialist. PPO plans generally don't require referrals. Always check your specific plan's rules, which a 1-800-MEDIGAP advisor can verify for you.

faqs.q: Can I see out-of-network doctors with a PPO?
faqs.a: Yes. A Medicare Advantage PPO covers out-of-network providers, but at a higher cost-share than in-network care. This flexibility is the main advantage of a PPO over an HMO. For the lowest cost, you'll still want to use in-network providers when possible.

faqs.q: Which is cheaper, HMO or PPO?
faqs.a: HMO plans are usually cheaper, with lower premiums and copays, because they restrict you to a network and require referrals. PPO plans cost more in exchange for out-of-network access and no referrals. Your total cost depends on how you use care. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for a comparison.

faqs.q: Which plan type is better for travelers?
faqs.a: A PPO is generally better for travelers because it covers out-of-network providers, giving you access to care in more places, though still at higher cost-share. HMOs cover only emergencies outside the network. If you travel often, you may also consider Original Medicare with Medigap. Ask 1-800-MEDIGAP.

page.metaDescription: What Medicare Advantage star ratings mean and how to use them in 2026. CMS rates plans 1-5 stars. Compare top plans with 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.quickAnswer: CMS rates every Medicare Advantage plan from 1 to 5 stars each year, measuring quality, member experience, customer service, and care management. Five stars is excellent and below 3 is below average. A 5-star plan also unlocks a special enrollment period to join almost any time of year.
page.intro: Star ratings are a free, objective quality tool. Here is what Medicare Advantage star ratings measure and how to use them.
page.heroSubhead: Find top-rated plans in your area with 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: What do star ratings measure?
sections.body: CMS assigns Medicare Advantage star ratings annually based on dozens of measures grouped into categories like preventive care and screenings, management of chronic conditions, member satisfaction, customer service, and the accuracy of the plan's drug pricing and complaint handling. Plans earn 1 to 5 stars, with 5 being excellent and anything under 3 considered below average. The ratings are published each fall before the Annual Election Period so you can factor quality into your choice. They're a rare apples-to-apples measure across plans. A 1-800-MEDIGAP advisor can show you the star ratings of every plan in your area.

sections.h2: Why star ratings matter when you choose
sections.body: Star ratings help you look past marketing and judge a plan on real performance. Higher-rated plans tend to deliver better care coordination, faster service, and fewer complaints, which matters most if you manage ongoing conditions. There's also a practical perk: if a 5-star plan is available in your area, you can use a special enrollment period to switch to it once between December 8 and November 30, outside the usual windows. Ratings can change yearly, so recheck each fall. To compare star ratings alongside cost and network for your local plans, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for a free review.

faqs.q: What do Medicare Advantage star ratings mean?
faqs.a: Star ratings are CMS quality scores from 1 to 5 stars, based on care quality, member experience, customer service, and care management. Five stars is excellent and below 3 is below average. They let you compare plans on real performance, published each fall before the Annual Election Period.

faqs.q: What is a good Medicare Advantage star rating?
faqs.a: A good rating is 4 stars or higher, indicating above-average quality and service. Five stars is excellent. Plans rated below 3 stars are considered below average and have flagged performance issues. A 1-800-MEDIGAP advisor can show the ratings for plans in your area.

faqs.q: Can I switch to a 5-star plan anytime?
faqs.a: Almost. If a 5-star Medicare Advantage plan is available in your area, you can use the 5-star Special Enrollment Period to switch to it once between December 8 and November 30, outside the normal windows. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to check whether a 5-star plan is offered near you.

faqs.q: How often are star ratings updated?
faqs.a: CMS updates Medicare Advantage star ratings once a year, releasing them each fall before the Annual Election Period. A plan's rating can rise or fall year to year, so it's wise to recheck your plan's current rating annually before deciding to keep or switch it.

faqs.q: Should I choose a plan based only on star ratings?
faqs.a: No. Star ratings are one important factor, but you should also confirm the plan covers your doctors and medications, compare the out-of-pocket maximum and premiums, and check extra benefits. A high rating means little if the plan doesn't fit your needs. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to weigh all factors.

page.metaDescription: How Medicare Advantage prior authorization works, what it affects, and how to appeal. Get help understanding your plan at 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.quickAnswer: Prior authorization is a Medicare Advantage rule requiring plan approval before it will cover certain services, like imaging, surgeries, or skilled nursing care. KFF reports it is the most common Advantage complaint, though new CMS rules for 2026 speed up decisions and limit some requirements.
page.intro: Prior authorization is the most-discussed Medicare Advantage rule. Here is what it is, what it affects, and how to handle it.

sections.h2: What is prior authorization?
sections.body: Prior authorization means your Medicare Advantage plan must approve a service before it agrees to pay for it. Plans commonly require it for higher-cost care such as advanced imaging (MRI, CT), planned surgeries, skilled nursing facility stays, certain specialist services, and some prescription drugs. The goal is to control costs and confirm care is medically necessary, but the process can delay treatment and frustrate patients and doctors. According to KFF, prior authorization is the leading complaint among Medicare Advantage members. Original Medicare rarely uses it. Knowing your plan's rules before you need care is essential, and a 1-800-MEDIGAP advisor can explain them clearly.

sections.h2: What to do if care is denied
sections.body: If your plan denies a prior authorization, you have the right to appeal, and many denials are overturned on appeal. Start by asking your doctor to submit supporting documentation, then follow your plan's formal appeal steps; you can escalate through multiple levels, including independent review. New CMS rules taking effect for 2026 require faster decisions and continuity of care when you switch plans, easing some delays. Still, prior authorization remains a key reason some seniors prefer Original Medicare with Medigap. If your plan's rules are causing problems, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to review whether a different plan or path fits you better.

faqs.q: What is prior authorization in Medicare Advantage?
faqs.a: Prior authorization is a requirement that your Medicare Advantage plan approve certain services, like imaging, surgery, or skilled nursing care, before it will cover them. It's meant to control costs and confirm medical necessity but can delay care. Original Medicare rarely requires it.

faqs.q: What services need prior authorization?
faqs.a: Plans most often require prior authorization for advanced imaging (MRI, CT scans), planned surgeries, skilled nursing facility stays, home health, certain specialist services, durable medical equipment, and some prescription drugs. Requirements vary by plan, so check your plan documents or ask a 1-800-MEDIGAP advisor.

faqs.q: Can I appeal a prior authorization denial?
faqs.a: Yes. You have the right to appeal a Medicare Advantage denial, and many denials are overturned. Ask your doctor to submit supporting records and follow your plan's appeal steps, which can escalate to independent review. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP if you need help understanding the process.

faqs.q: Does Original Medicare require prior authorization?
faqs.a: Rarely. Original Medicare requires prior authorization for only a limited set of services, far fewer than Medicare Advantage. This is one reason some seniors who want fewer access hurdles choose Original Medicare with a Medigap policy. A 1-800-MEDIGAP advisor can compare both approaches for you.

faqs.q: Are prior authorization rules changing in 2026?
faqs.a: Yes. New CMS rules taking effect for 2026 require Medicare Advantage plans to make faster prior-authorization decisions and provide continuity of care when members switch plans, aiming to reduce harmful delays. The rules don't eliminate prior authorization but seek to streamline it. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for details.

page.metaDescription: $0 premium Medicare Advantage plans explained: what they really cost and the catch. Compare free at 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.quickAnswer: A $0 premium Medicare Advantage plan charges no monthly plan premium, but it is not free: you still pay your Part B premium ($185 in 2026 for most people), plus copays, coinsurance, and deductibles when you use care, up to the plan's annual out-of-pocket maximum.
page.intro: $0 premium plans are popular but widely misunderstood. Here is what they really cost and how to evaluate them.

sections.h2: What '$0 premium' really means
sections.body: A $0 premium Medicare Advantage plan simply means the insurer charges no separate monthly premium for the plan itself. It does not mean free healthcare. You must still pay your monthly Part B premium ($185 in 2026 for most people), and you pay copays, coinsurance, and any deductibles each time you receive care. These costs accumulate until you hit the plan's annual out-of-pocket maximum. Insurers can offer $0 premiums because CMS pays them to cover your benefits. The label can be misleading if it leads you to ignore the real out-of-pocket costs. A 1-800-MEDIGAP advisor shows you a plan's full cost picture, not just the premium.

sections.h2: How to judge a $0 premium plan
sections.body: Don't choose a plan on the premium alone; a $0 plan with high copays can cost more than a low-premium plan with better cost-sharing. Compare the total estimated annual cost based on your expected care: look at copays for primary and specialist visits, hospital stays, and drugs, the deductible, and the out-of-pocket maximum. Confirm your doctors are in-network and your medications are on the formulary. Check the CMS star rating too. Done right, a $0 premium plan can be an excellent value, but only when the numbers fit your situation. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for a true cost comparison.

faqs.q: Are $0 premium Medicare Advantage plans really free?
faqs.a: No. A $0 premium plan charges no monthly plan premium, but you still pay your Part B premium ($185 in 2026 for most people) plus copays and coinsurance when you use care. The $0 refers only to the plan premium, not your total healthcare costs.

faqs.q: How can plans offer a $0 premium?
faqs.a: Insurers can offer $0 premium Medicare Advantage plans because CMS pays them a fixed amount per enrollee to provide benefits. That federal funding lets the plan waive its monthly premium while still covering your care, recouping costs through copays and managed networks. It's a real, legitimate option.

faqs.q: Is a $0 premium plan a good deal?
faqs.a: It can be, but not always. A $0 premium plan with high copays may cost more overall than a low-premium plan with better cost-sharing. Judge it by total estimated annual cost, network, drug coverage, and star rating, not the premium alone. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for a real comparison.

faqs.q: Do I still pay the Part B premium with a $0 plan?
faqs.a: Yes. You must keep paying your Part B premium ($185 in 2026 for most people) even on a $0 premium Medicare Advantage plan. Some plans offer a giveback benefit that reduces this premium, which a 1-800-MEDIGAP advisor can help you find.

faqs.q: What costs do I still pay on a $0 premium plan?
faqs.a: You pay your Part B premium, plus copays, coinsurance, and deductibles when you use care, until you reach the plan's annual out-of-pocket maximum ($9,250 in-network in 2026). Drug costs follow the plan's formulary. Total costs depend on how much care you use during the year.

children.keyword: medicare advantage out of pocket maximum 2026

page.metaDescription: Medicare Advantage out-of-pocket maximum for 2026 explained. Know your in-network cap and how it protects you. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.quickAnswer: Every Medicare Advantage plan must cap your in-network out-of-pocket spending each year. For 2026, CMS sets the federal maximum at $9,250 for in-network services, with a higher combined cap for plans that also cover out-of-network care. Many plans set lower limits than the federal ceiling.
page.intro: The out-of-pocket maximum is one of Medicare Advantage's biggest advantages. Here is how the 2026 cap protects you.

sections.h2: How the out-of-pocket maximum works
sections.body: The out-of-pocket maximum is the most you'll pay for covered medical services in a year before your Medicare Advantage plan covers 100% of further in-network costs. For 2026, CMS caps the in-network maximum at $9,250, though many plans choose lower limits to compete. Plans with out-of-network benefits (like PPOs) have a separate, higher combined cap. The maximum counts your copays, coinsurance, and deductibles for medical care, but not your monthly premiums or most prescription drug spending, which has its own separate cap under Part D. This protection is a key reason people choose Advantage. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to compare caps.

sections.h2: Why the cap matters
sections.body: Original Medicare has no out-of-pocket maximum, so a serious illness could leave you with unlimited bills unless you add a Medigap policy. Medicare Advantage builds in a cap, giving you a predictable worst-case for the year. Because plans set their own limits below the federal ceiling, a lower out-of-pocket maximum can be worth more than a slightly lower premium if you expect significant care. When comparing plans, always check both the in-network cap and, for PPOs, the combined cap. A licensed advisor at 1-800-MEDIGAP can line up the out-of-pocket maximums of your local plans so you choose the strongest protection for your budget.

faqs.q: What is the Medicare Advantage out-of-pocket maximum for 2026?
faqs.a: For 2026, CMS sets the federal in-network out-of-pocket maximum at $9,250 for Medicare Advantage plans, with a higher combined cap for plans covering out-of-network care. Many plans set lower limits to compete. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare the caps on plans in your area.

faqs.q: What counts toward the out-of-pocket maximum?
faqs.a: Your copays, coinsurance, and deductibles for covered medical services count toward the out-of-pocket maximum. Monthly premiums do not count, and prescription drug costs have a separate cap under Part D. Once you hit the maximum, the plan covers 100% of further covered in-network care.

faqs.q: Does Original Medicare have an out-of-pocket maximum?
faqs.a: No. Original Medicare has no annual out-of-pocket maximum, so costs can be unlimited without supplemental coverage. This is a major advantage of Medicare Advantage, which always includes a cap. Original Medicare beneficiaries typically add a Medigap policy for similar protection. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare.

faqs.q: Do prescription drugs count toward the maximum?
faqs.a: No. Prescription drug spending does not count toward the medical out-of-pocket maximum. Drugs have their own separate annual cap under Part D, which improved in recent years to limit what you pay for medications. Your medical and drug spending are tracked separately. Ask 1-800-MEDIGAP for details.

faqs.q: Can a plan have a lower out-of-pocket maximum than the federal cap?
faqs.a: Yes. The $9,250 in-network figure is the maximum a plan can set for 2026, but many plans choose lower limits to attract members. A lower cap means stronger financial protection. When comparing plans, check each one's specific maximum. A 1-800-MEDIGAP advisor can line them up for you.

page.metaDescription: The Medicare Advantage giveback benefit can reduce your Part B premium. See how it works and who qualifies. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.quickAnswer: The Medicare Advantage giveback benefit is a feature of certain plans that reimburses part or all of your monthly Part B premium ($185 in 2026 for most people). The plan pays a set amount back, lowering what's deducted from your Social Security check. Availability and amounts vary by ZIP code.
page.intro: The giveback benefit can put money back in your pocket each month. Here is how the Part B premium reduction works.
page.heroSubhead: See if a giveback plan is available near you, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: How the giveback benefit works
sections.body: The giveback benefit, officially the Part B premium reduction, is offered by some Medicare Advantage plans that agree to pay a portion of your monthly Part B premium back to you. Because most people have their $185 (2026) Part B premium deducted from Social Security, a giveback reduces that deduction, effectively raising your monthly check. Amounts range from a few dollars to the full premium, depending on the plan and your area. Not all plans offer it, and where they do, the giveback is one feature among many to weigh. To find out whether a giveback plan is available in your ZIP code, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

sections.h2: Should you choose a plan for the giveback?
sections.body: A giveback can be valuable, but don't pick a plan for the giveback alone. A plan offering a large Part B reduction might have a narrower network, fewer extra benefits, or higher copays that outweigh the savings. Always compare the whole package: confirm your doctors and drugs are covered, check the out-of-pocket maximum and star rating, then factor in the giveback as a bonus. Used wisely, a giveback plan that also fits your care needs is a great value. A licensed advisor at 1-800-MEDIGAP can show you which local giveback plans actually make sense for your situation, free of charge.

faqs.q: What is the Medicare Advantage giveback benefit?
faqs.a: The giveback benefit, or Part B premium reduction, is a feature of some Medicare Advantage plans that pays part or all of your monthly Part B premium ($185 in 2026 for most people) back to you, lowering what's deducted from your Social Security check. Availability varies by ZIP code.

faqs.q: How much money can the giveback benefit save?
faqs.a: Giveback amounts range from a few dollars to the full Part B premium each month, depending on the plan and your area. The reduction lowers what Social Security deducts, effectively increasing your monthly check. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to see what giveback amounts are available near you.

faqs.q: Who qualifies for the giveback benefit?
faqs.a: To qualify, you must be enrolled in a Medicare Advantage plan that offers the Part B giveback and pay your own Part B premium. People whose premium is already paid by Medicaid or a state program generally can't benefit. A 1-800-MEDIGAP advisor can confirm whether you qualify.

faqs.q: Is the giveback benefit available everywhere?
faqs.a: No. Giveback plans are offered only in certain areas and by certain insurers, and availability is set by ZIP code and county. Some regions have several giveback plans; others have none. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP with your ZIP code to find out whether a giveback plan is offered where you live.

faqs.q: Should I choose a plan just for the giveback?
faqs.a: No. Don't choose a plan for the giveback alone. A large premium reduction may come with a narrower network or higher copays that erase the savings. Compare the full plan, network, drugs, out-of-pocket maximum, and star rating, with the giveback as a bonus. Ask 1-800-MEDIGAP to help.

page.metaDescription: How Medicare Advantage covers dental, vision, and hearing benefits Original Medicare won't. Compare plan extras with 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.h1: Medicare Advantage Dental, Vision, and Hearing
page.quickAnswer: Most Medicare Advantage plans include dental, vision, and hearing benefits that Original Medicare does not cover, such as routine cleanings, exams, glasses allowances, and hearing aids. Coverage levels, annual allowances, and provider networks vary widely by plan, so the details matter as much as the benefit.
page.intro: Dental, vision, and hearing are top reasons seniors choose Medicare Advantage. Here is how these extra benefits actually work.
page.heroSubhead: Compare plans with the best extras at 1-800-MEDIGAP.

sections.h2: What dental, vision, and hearing benefits cover
sections.body: Original Medicare generally excludes routine dental, vision, and hearing care, a major gap for seniors. Most Medicare Advantage plans fill it by adding these benefits. Dental coverage can include cleanings, exams, X-rays, and sometimes major work like crowns or dentures up to an annual allowance. Vision benefits often cover routine eye exams plus an allowance toward glasses or contacts. Hearing benefits typically cover exams and a substantial allowance toward hearing aids, which can otherwise cost thousands. The depth varies a lot between plans, so a generous-sounding benefit may have low annual limits. A 1-800-MEDIGAP advisor can compare the actual dollar amounts for you.

sections.h2: How to compare the extras
sections.body: Because dental, vision, and hearing benefits differ so much, read past the headline. Check the annual maximum allowance for each, whether there's a deductible or waiting period, and whether you must use specific in-network dentists, optometrists, or audiologists. A plan advertising 'comprehensive dental' might cap coverage at a few hundred dollars, while another offers far more. If these benefits are a priority, especially expensive hearing aids or major dental work, the specifics can outweigh a difference in premium. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) and a licensed advisor will line up the real dental, vision, and hearing allowances of plans in your area.

faqs.q: Does Medicare Advantage cover dental, vision, and hearing?
faqs.a: Yes, most Medicare Advantage plans include dental, vision, and hearing benefits that Original Medicare doesn't cover, such as cleanings, eye exams, glasses allowances, and hearing aids. Coverage levels and annual allowances vary widely by plan, so compare the specifics. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for help.

faqs.q: Does Original Medicare cover dental or hearing aids?
faqs.a: No. Original Medicare generally does not cover routine dental care, eyeglasses, or hearing aids. This gap is a major reason many seniors choose Medicare Advantage, which usually adds these benefits, or buy separate dental and vision plans. A 1-800-MEDIGAP advisor can explain your options.

faqs.q: How much dental coverage do Medicare Advantage plans offer?
faqs.a: It varies widely. Some plans cover only preventive cleanings and exams, while others add an annual allowance, often a few hundred to a couple thousand dollars, toward major work like crowns or dentures. Check each plan's annual maximum and network. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare amounts.

faqs.q: Do Medicare Advantage plans cover hearing aids?
faqs.a: Many do. Most Medicare Advantage plans with hearing benefits cover hearing exams and provide an allowance toward hearing aids, which can cost thousands out of pocket otherwise. Allowances and in-network provider requirements differ by plan. A 1-800-MEDIGAP advisor can find plans with strong hearing coverage near you.

faqs.q: Are the dental and vision networks restricted?
faqs.a: Often yes. Many Medicare Advantage plans require you to use in-network dentists, optometrists, and audiologists to get full coverage. Using out-of-network providers may cost more or not be covered. Always confirm your preferred providers participate, which a 1-800-MEDIGAP advisor can verify before you enroll.
